content:
13 min read
Just now

--

There is a line of code you have written a thousand times and never once looked at.
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
process(arr[i]);
}We call it O(n). We nod. We move on. It is the most boring line in the language, and for years it was boring to me too. Then one afternoon it stopped being boring, and I have not fully recovered.
The question that did it is embarrassingly simple. Why is there no data structure that lets you walk an array in less than O(n)? Not search it. Not jump to an index. Actually touch every element, do something to each one, but somehow not pay a price that grows with how many there are. Sorting has a famous floor. You cannot comparison-sort faster than N log N, and everybody accepts that as a law of nature. So I wanted to know: is iteration hiding a floor of its own that nobody bothered to point at, or is it just that no one had been clever enough to build the structure that beats it?
Here is the part I want to be honest about before anything else. This was never a research project. It was an itch. The specific, maddening kind of itch where you suspect the clean answer everyone repeats is covering for something, and you would genuinely rather claw at the wall than accept the abstraction and go ship the feature like a normal person.
So let me take you through the clawing, because the clawing is the whole point.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

First I tried to cheat the count
The reflex any engineer has is to blame the shape of the data. If the loop is slow, restructure. Trees, hash maps, skip lists, some exotic index nobody has named yet. Surely some arrangement lets you visit everything without paying per element.
This is where I hit the first version of the wall, and it took me longer than I would like to admit to stop arguing with it.
You can move the cost. You cannot delete it.
If the problem genuinely requires that N distinct things get processed, then N things have to happen. You can make each one cheaper. You can reorder them. You can hide them behind a prettier API. But the count N is a property of the problem, not of your code. Any structure that seems to “avoid” visiting N elements is really just a problem where you never needed all N to begin with. That is a different problem wearing your problem’s clothes.
And the moment I got cute and thought “fine, build hardware that touches all N cells at once,” I realized I had not escaped anything. I had just moved O(n) out of the time budget and into the wire budget. Now I need O(n) lanes, or gates, or physical channels. The complexity did not evaporate. It changed which account it was billed to. Time got cheaper and area, energy, and heat got more expensive by exactly the amount I thought I had saved.
That was lesson one, and it turned out to be the entire story in miniature. In these problems the cost is never destroyed. It is relocated. Keep that sentence in your pocket, because everything after this is just me learning it again in more expensive ways.
So I tried to stop counting altogether
Here is where I got genuinely excited, and I still think the excitement was earned.
What if the sequential framing is the mistake itself? A for-loop is one pointer walking a line, one thing at a time, because that is what a pointer is. But think about radio. A tower does not call each listener one by one. It sends a single signal and a million receivers get it at the same instant. The sender pays O(1). That is not iteration. That is broadcast.
So the idea was: what if touching N things could work like a wave instead of a walk? One signal, released once, washing over everything simultaneously. Light, radio, some physical field doing the fan-out for free. For a good while I was convinced this was the door.
Now tell me that is not a reasonable thing to believe. It looks airtight. It is also wrong, and the reason it is wrong is the most important thing I learned in this entire chase.
The wall that is not made of silicon
“N places now know something new” and “fewer than N transfer events happened” are contradictory. Not hard. Not unsolved. Contradictory, in the way a married bachelor is contradictory.
The radio tower feels like O(1), but look closer. The sender’s cost is O(1), one broadcast, sure. But the total work in the system is still O(n), because a million receivers each independently perform a receiving event. The energy, the state change, the physical “now I hold this information” happens N times in N places. Radio did not beat O(n). It parallelized it and made the sender’s slice O(1). Those are different claims, and I had been quietly swapping one for the other every time I got excited.
This split has a name I did not know at the time. Work versus span. Work is how many operations happen across the whole system. Span is the length of the longest chain you cannot avoid waiting through, the elapsed time if you had infinite parallel hands. Broadcast annihilates span. It does nothing to work. The N events still occur. They just occur at once instead of in a queue.
And when I pushed past radio into “okay, but quantum, surely you can copy information for free somewhere in the physics,” I ran straight into the formal, proven version of the wall. The no-cloning theorem, from 1982, says you cannot duplicate an arbitrary unknown quantum state. Its stronger sibling, the no-broadcasting theorem from 1996, closes the door I was leaning on by name. You cannot fan an unknown state out to N parties for free. The universe has a specific rule against the specific shortcut I wanted.
That reframed the whole hunt. I was not staring at an unsolved engineering problem. I was staring at an information-theoretic floor. Sit with how different those two things are, because I refused to for weeks.
The worst part was that the door already had a name
There is a particular humiliation in thinking you have found a secret passage and then noticing the handle is worn smooth by every hand that came before yours.
The broadcast trick I “discovered”? That is the Observer pattern, and at scale it is pub-sub. Older than me. The work-versus-span thing I proudly re-derived? That is the PRAM model, foundational to all of parallel computing. The copy-for-free idea? Closed by no-cloning. The use-light-instead-of-electrons idea? That is photonic computing, a funded field with conferences.
Every stop on my map already had a signpost. I just could not read the language until I had walked there myself.
I will be honest, that stung, and then it stopped stinging, because I realized I was measuring the wrong thing. The question was never “did I discover something new.” I did not. The real signal is that a naive first-principles itch kept depositing me at exactly the questions other people found important enough to build formal machinery around. Arriving at a famous question by yourself is not a discovery. It is also not nothing. It means your nose points somewhere real, even when your hands are empty.
So I kept going. Because there was one move left, and it was the one I actually cared about.
The proof that was sitting there waiting for me
It turns out my exact question, can you use something like superposition to touch an unsorted structure faster than one at a time, is not open. It was answered before I was writing code, and the answer is gorgeous and specific.
Grover’s algorithm, 1996, does almost precisely the thing I was fantasizing about. Give it an unsorted space of K elements. A classical search needs up to O(K) checks because it walks the line. Grover uses superposition to do it in O(√K). That is a real speedup, not marketing. Search a million-item space in about a thousand steps.
My reaction was exactly the one you are having. Great, if √K is on the table, why stop there, push it to O(1), finish the job.
And that is where the proof was waiting.
In 1997, four people named Bennett, Bernstein, Brassard, and Vazirani proved that no quantum algorithm can beat O(√K) for unstructured search. Not “we have not found a faster one.” Proven that a faster one cannot exist. √K is not the current record. It is the ceiling.
Here is the distinction that I think is worth more than the entire rest of this piece, so I am going to say it slowly.
There is a difference between a problem that is hard to engineer and a problem that is proven impossible.
Hard to engineer means we do not know how yet, the pieces have not come together, throw time and money and cleverness at it. This is where flight sat. This is where AI sat for decades. Nobody had a proof those things were impossible. They just had doubt, and doubt is a statement about imagination, not about reality.
Proven impossible means someone has shown, with a proof, that no future hardware and no unborn genius changes the answer. Trisecting an arbitrary angle with only a compass and straightedge is proven impossible. Wantzel settled it in 1837. It is not waiting on a better mathematician. It is done.
O(1) unstructured search is the second kind. So is free broadcast to N parties. They are not unfinished. They are closed, for the same species of reason that sorting cannot beat N log N.
I hated this. I want you to feel free to hate it too. Then keep reading, because the physics gets very good.
But objects can be in two places at once now
This is the part that kept my hope alive, and I do not think the hope was foolish.
In actual labs, right now, this is happening. I went and read the papers instead of trusting a summary, and here is what they say.
Sodium nanoparticles containing over 7,000 atoms, with masses above 170,000 daltons, were placed into a spatial superposition, effectively existing across two locations about 133 nanometers apart until measured. That is Nature, 2026, and it is one of the largest objects ever demonstrably smeared across two places at once. Separately, in Nature Physics in 2024, atoms were held in a spatial superposition, each one existing as two coherent wave packets in two locations, for as long as 70 seconds. Seventy seconds is a geological era in this field.
Read those and tell me your hypothesis machine does not start spinning. Mine did. Here is exactly what it produced, and I am going to state it as confidently as I argued it at the time, because I did argue it, hard.
If an object can be proven to sit in multiple places at the same time, then build a system that uses light instead of binary for a few of its operations. A for-loop crawls one index at a time because a pointer can only be in one place at once. But if a light particle can genuinely be at many positions simultaneously, then a pointer built from that particle traverses a K-length array in O(1). One read. Every position. At once.
I know how science-fiction that sounds. I also know AI sounded like science fiction inside living memory, so do not hand me the “that is impossible” face too quickly. That was my whole argument, and I meant it.
Here is exactly where my own hypothesis dies
It does not die from a hardware limitation. It dies from a single sentence of physics, and once you see it you cannot unsee it.
A superposition is one system in an indefinite state spread across locations, described by a single wavefunction. It is not N independent copies of definite, readable information sitting at N addresses. My hypothesis quietly assumed the second thing while the universe only offers the first.
The instant you measure it, and you must measure it or you get no answer out of the machine, it collapses to one outcome, at one location, chosen at random by the probabilities. You do not read all K positions. You read one index, this run. Roll again, get another. That is not a flaw in today’s equipment that a smarter lab fixes next year. It is the measurement postulate of quantum mechanics itself. It is the same rule that forced that beautiful nanoparticle experiment to rebuild its result out of many repeated runs stacking into an interference pattern, rather than reading the answer out i
